problem text: There are 6 swimmers of equal skill, named $1-6$, competing in a race. Determine the likelihood that swimmer $2$ finishes in $2$nd position and swimmer $5$ secures a position within the top $3$.
problem solution: Given that all swimmers have the same level of skill, and due to their interchangeability, each participant lands in any of the $6$ positions with a chance of $\dfrac{1}{6}$. The chance that swimmer $2$ secures the $2$nd position is $\dfrac{1}{6}$. With this condition, $2$ positions remain in the top $3$ for swimmer $5$ to occupy. Since swimmer $2$ occupies one spot, the chance that swimmer $5$ is within the top $3$ is $\dfrac{2}{5}$. Thus, the final result is $\dfrac{1}{6} \cdot \dfrac{2}{5} = \dfrac{1}{15}$.
problem hint: As each swimmer has equal skill, by the principle of exchangeability, each has a probability of $\dfrac{1}{6}$ to finish in any of the $6$ positions.
